protocols.description: The Authorization Code flow with Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE) is the RECOMMENDED grant type for all OAuth 2.0 clients, including public clients such as single-page applications and native mobile apps, as well as confidential clients. The client generates a cryptographically random code_verifier and derives a code_challenge (using S256 transformation) before redirecting the user to the authorization server. After user authentication and consent, the authorization server returns an authorization code to the client's redirect URI. The client then exchanges this code at the token endpoint along with the original code_verifier. The authorization server verifies the code_challenge matches the code_verifier before issuing tokens. This flow eliminates the need to embed client secrets in public clients and prevents authorization code interception attacks. OAuth 2.1 mandates PKCE for all authorization code grants regardless of client type.
protocols.security_considerations: PKCE mitigates authorization code interception attacks (IETF RFC 7636) by binding the code exchange to the original client session via the code_verifier/code_challenge pair. Always use the S256 challenge method; the plain method offers no meaningful security benefit and should be rejected by the authorization server. Ensure the code_verifier has at least 256 bits of entropy (43-128 unreserved characters per RFC 7636 Section 4.1). The redirect URI must be registered exactly (no wildcards) to prevent open redirect attacks. Use state parameters as CSRF protection even with PKCE. Token responses should use Cache-Control: no-store and Pragma: no-cache headers. For SPAs, consider using the Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) pattern to keep tokens out of the browser entirely. Validate the issuer (iss) claim in token responses per RFC 9207 to prevent mix-up attacks.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  AuthZ Server             Resource Server
  |                          |                          |
  |--generate code_verifier--|
  |--derive code_challenge---|
  |                          |                          |
  |---AuthZ Request--------->|                          |
  |  (code_challenge,        |                          |
  |   code_challenge_method, |                          |
  |   state, redirect_uri)   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |    User authenticates    |                          |
  |    and grants consent    |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--AuthZ Code + state-----|                          |
  |  (via redirect_uri)      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---Token Request--------->|                          |
  |  (code, code_verifier,   |                          |
  |   redirect_uri)          |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Access Token + --------|                          |
  |   Refresh Token          |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ Token------------------------>|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|

protocols.description: The Client Credentials grant is designed for machine-to-machine (M2M) communication where no end-user is involved. The client authenticates directly with the authorization server using its own credentials (typically client_id and client_secret, or a client assertion such as a signed JWT per RFC 7523). The authorization server validates the client's identity and returns an access token scoped to the client's permissions. This flow is commonly used for backend service-to-service calls, daemon processes, batch jobs, and microservice architectures. Since there is no user context, the access token represents the application identity rather than a delegated user authorization. The client should request only the minimum scopes necessary for the intended operation.
protocols.security_considerations: Client secrets must be stored securely using hardware security modules (HSMs), secret managers (e.g., Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault), or environment variables with restricted access. Prefer mutual TLS (RFC 8705) or private_key_jwt (RFC 7523) for client authentication over shared secrets, as these provide non-repudiation and are not susceptible to secret leakage. Rotate client secrets on a regular schedule and immediately upon suspected compromise. Issued access tokens should have short lifetimes (5-15 minutes) since there is no refresh token mechanism in this flow. Apply network-level controls (IP allowlisting, mTLS) in addition to OAuth scopes to limit blast radius. Monitor for anomalous token request patterns that may indicate credential compromise. Never issue refresh tokens for client credentials grants.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client (Service)        AuthZ Server             Resource Server
  |                          |                          |
  |---Token Request--------->|                          |
  |  (grant_type=            |                          |
  |   client_credentials,    |                          |
  |   client_id,             |                          |
  |   client_secret,         |                          |
  |   scope)                 |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Access Token-----------|                          |
  |  (token_type=Bearer,     |                          |
  |   expires_in, scope)     |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ Token------------------------>|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|

protocols.description: The Device Authorization Grant (Device Code flow) enables OAuth authorization on input-constrained devices that lack a browser or have limited input capabilities, such as smart TVs, IoT devices, CLI tools, and gaming consoles. The client requests a device code and a user code from the authorization server's device authorization endpoint. The device displays the user code and a verification URI to the user. The user navigates to the verification URI on a secondary device (e.g., smartphone or laptop), enters the user code, authenticates, and grants consent. Meanwhile, the client polls the authorization server's token endpoint at a specified interval until the user completes authorization, at which point the server returns an access token and optionally a refresh token. The polling interval and device code expiration are specified in the initial device authorization response.
protocols.security_considerations: The device code has a limited lifetime (typically 300-600 seconds) and should expire if not used. The authorization server must enforce the polling interval (typically 5 seconds) and return slow_down errors for clients that poll too aggressively. Implement rate limiting on the device authorization endpoint to prevent abuse. The user code should be short enough for easy entry but long enough to prevent brute-force guessing (8 characters with a restricted character set is typical). Display the complete verification URI including the user code as a QR code when possible to reduce phishing risk. Be aware of remote phishing attacks where an attacker initiates the device flow and tricks a user into authorizing their device code. Consider binding the device code to a specific device fingerprint where possible. The verification URI should use HTTPS and be on a well-known domain.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Device Client           AuthZ Server             User (2nd Device)
  |                          |                          |
  |---Device AuthZ Request-->|                          |
  |  (client_id, scope)      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Device Code, User------|                          |
  |   Code, Verification URI |                          |
  |   Interval, Expires_in   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |--Display user_code &-----|------------------------->|
  |  verification_uri        |                          |
  |                          |    Navigate to URI       |
  |                          |<--Enter user_code--------|  
  |                          |    Authenticate + Consent|
  |                          |                          |
  |---Poll Token Endpoint--->|                          |
  |  (device_code,           |                          |
  |   grant_type=            |                          |
  |   device_code)           |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--authorization_pending--|                          |
  |  (keep polling)          |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---Poll Token Endpoint--->|                          |
  |<--Access Token-----------|                          |

protocols.description: The Refresh Token grant allows clients to obtain new access tokens without requiring the user to re-authenticate. When an access token expires, the client presents its refresh token to the authorization server's token endpoint to receive a fresh access token and, optionally, a new refresh token (refresh token rotation). Refresh tokens are typically long-lived (days to months) but should be constrained by absolute and idle timeout policies. This flow is essential for maintaining seamless user sessions in applications that require long-lived access, such as mobile apps and desktop applications. The authorization server may reduce the scope of the new access token compared to the original grant, but cannot expand it. Confidential clients must authenticate when using refresh tokens.
protocols.security_considerations: Refresh tokens are high-value credentials and must be stored securely. For native and mobile apps, use platform-specific secure storage (iOS Keychain, Android Keystore). For web applications, store refresh tokens server-side and never expose them to the browser. Implement refresh token rotation (issue a new refresh token with each use and invalidate the old one) to detect token theft via replay detection. If a previously used refresh token is presented, revoke the entire token family (all tokens issued from that grant) as this indicates potential compromise. Set absolute lifetime limits on refresh tokens (e.g., 90 days) to bound the window of compromise. Implement idle timeout (e.g., 24 hours) to revoke tokens not actively used. Bind refresh tokens to the client via proof-of-possession mechanisms (DPoP per RFC 9449) where supported. Monitor refresh token usage for anomalies such as geographical impossibility or device fingerprint changes.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  AuthZ Server             Resource Server
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ Token------------------------>|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--401 Unauthorized (expired)----------------------|
  |                          |                          |
  |---Refresh Token Request->|                          |
  |  (grant_type=            |                          |
  |   refresh_token,         |                          |
  |   refresh_token,         |                          |
  |   client_id,             |                          |
  |   [client_secret])       |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--New Access Token +-----|                          |
  |   New Refresh Token      |                          |
  |   (old RT invalidated)   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ New Token-------------------->|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|

protocols.description: The original Authorization Code grant (without PKCE) is the traditional server-side flow for confidential clients. The client redirects the user-agent to the authorization server with a client_id, requested scope, state parameter, and redirect_uri. After user authentication and consent, the authorization server redirects back to the client's redirect_uri with an authorization code. The client then exchanges this code at the token endpoint, authenticating with its client_secret. While still functional, this flow is considered legacy for new implementations because it relies on a static client_secret for code exchange security, which does not protect against authorization code interception in scenarios where the redirect can be observed. OAuth 2.1 requires PKCE for all authorization code grants, making this variant without PKCE effectively deprecated for new deployments.
protocols.security_considerations: Without PKCE, this flow is vulnerable to authorization code interception attacks, particularly on mobile platforms where custom URI schemes can be registered by multiple apps (per IETF RFC 7636 Section 1). The client_secret must be kept confidential and should never be embedded in client-side code, which limits this flow to server-side applications with secure backends. Always validate the state parameter to prevent CSRF attacks. Use exact redirect_uri matching (no wildcards or pattern matching) at the authorization server. The authorization code should be short-lived (typically 30-60 seconds) and single-use. Migrate to authorization-code-pkce for all new implementations. For existing deployments, add PKCE support as a backward-compatible enhancement. Implement Authorization Server Issuer Identification (RFC 9207) to prevent mix-up attacks in multi-provider scenarios.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client (Server)         AuthZ Server             Resource Server
  |                          |                          |
  |---AuthZ Request--------->|                          |
  |  (client_id, scope,      |                          |
  |   state, redirect_uri)   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |    User authenticates    |                          |
  |    and grants consent    |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--AuthZ Code + state-----|                          |
  |  (via redirect_uri)      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---Token Request--------->|                          |
  |  (code, client_id,       |                          |
  |   client_secret,         |                          |
  |   redirect_uri)          |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Access Token + --------|                          |
  |   Refresh Token          |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ Token------------------------>|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|

protocols.description: DEPRECATED. The Implicit grant was originally designed for browser-based (JavaScript) clients that could not securely store a client secret. The authorization server returns the access token directly in the URL fragment of the redirect URI, bypassing the token endpoint entirely. This flow is now deprecated by the OAuth Security Best Current Practice (RFC 9700) and OAuth 2.1 because it exposes access tokens in the browser history, HTTP Referer headers, and server logs. The access token is visible to any JavaScript running on the page, making it vulnerable to cross-site scripting (XSS) token exfiltration. Additionally, the implicit flow does not support refresh tokens, forcing repeated full authorization flows. All new browser-based applications should use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE instead, preferably with a Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) pattern.
protocols.security_considerations: DO NOT USE for new implementations. The access token in the URL fragment is exposed to: (1) browser history and bookmarks, (2) HTTP Referer headers leaked to third-party resources, (3) server access logs if the fragment is inadvertently captured, (4) any JavaScript on the page including injected scripts via XSS. There is no mechanism to verify that the token was issued to the requesting client (no client authentication at the token endpoint), enabling token substitution attacks. The flow lacks sender-constrained tokens (no PKCE, no DPoP). Access tokens cannot be kept confidential from the resource owner's user-agent. Refresh tokens are explicitly prohibited, leading to poor user experience as tokens expire. Migration path: replace with Authorization Code + PKCE, or better yet, use BFF pattern to keep tokens server-side entirely.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client (Browser SPA)    AuthZ Server             Resource Server
  |                          |                          |
  |---AuthZ Request--------->|                          |
  |  (response_type=token,   |                          |
  |   client_id, scope,      |                          |
  |   state, redirect_uri)   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |    User authenticates    |                          |
  |    and grants consent    |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Redirect w/ Token------|                          |
  |  (access_token in URL    |                          |
  |   fragment #)            |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [TOKEN EXPOSED in       |                          |
  |   browser history,       |                          |
  |   Referer headers,       |                          |
  |   logs -- INSECURE]      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ Token------------------------>|
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|

protocols.description: DEPRECATED. The Resource Owner Password Credentials (ROPC) grant allows the client to collect the user's username and password directly and exchange them for tokens at the authorization server's token endpoint. This completely undermines the core OAuth principle of delegated authorization, where the user should only enter credentials at the authorization server, never at the client. The ROPC grant was originally included for migration scenarios from legacy direct-authentication systems, but it is now deprecated by OAuth 2.1 and the OAuth Security BCP (RFC 9700). This flow trains users to enter credentials in untrusted applications, cannot support multi-factor authentication flows that require interactive challenges, and gives the client access to the plaintext password. It should only be considered as a last resort for legacy system migration with a concrete plan to move to authorization code + PKCE.
protocols.security_considerations: DO NOT USE for new implementations. Critical security issues: (1) The client receives and handles the user's plaintext password, violating the principle of least privilege and creating a credential exposure vector. (2) Users are trained to enter passwords in third-party applications, making them vulnerable to credential phishing. (3) The flow cannot accommodate interactive MFA challenges (TOTP, WebAuthn, push notification) because there is no user-agent redirect. (4) Password changes at the authorization server do not propagate to clients that have cached credentials. (5) There is no mechanism for consent or scope approval by the user. (6) The client must store or transmit the password, increasing the attack surface for credential theft. Migration path: implement Authorization Code + PKCE with the authorization server handling all credential input. For headless/CLI use cases, use the Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) instead.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  AuthZ Server             Resource Server
  |                          |                          |
  |  [User enters username   |                          |
  |   and password in the    |                          |
  |   CLIENT -- INSECURE]    |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---Token Request--------->|                          |
  |  (grant_type=password,   |                          |
  |   username, password,    |                          |
  |   client_id,             |                          |
  |   [client_secret],       |                          |
  |   scope)                 |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Access Token + --------|                          |
  |   Refresh Token          |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ Token------------------------>|
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|

protocols.description: Token Exchange (RFC 8693) enables a client to request a security token from an authorization server by presenting an existing token (the subject_token) and optionally an actor_token. This is essential for cross-domain delegation, impersonation, and token transformation scenarios in microservice architectures. The client sends a token exchange request specifying the subject_token (the identity being acted upon), the subject_token_type (e.g., access_token, id_token, jwt, saml2), and optionally an actor_token representing the entity performing the exchange. The authorization server validates the input tokens, applies authorization policies, and issues a new token appropriate for the target audience or resource. Common use cases include: a frontend BFF exchanging an authorization code for a downstream-scoped token, a microservice requesting a narrowly-scoped token for a downstream call, and cross-protocol bridges such as exchanging a SAML assertion for an OAuth access token.
protocols.security_considerations: Token exchange introduces delegation chains that must be carefully controlled. The authorization server must validate the subject_token and actor_token signatures, expiration, audience, and issuer before issuing a new token. Implement scope reduction (the exchanged token should have equal or fewer privileges than the input token) to prevent privilege escalation. Track delegation depth to prevent infinite exchange chains. The may_act claim (RFC 8693 Section 4.4) should be verified when present to ensure the actor is authorized to act on behalf of the subject. Log all token exchange operations for audit and anomaly detection. Restrict which clients are permitted to perform token exchanges through authorization server policy. Consider adding the act claim to issued tokens to preserve the delegation chain for downstream services. Validate the audience restriction of exchanged tokens to prevent token reuse across unintended services. Rate-limit token exchange requests per client to prevent abuse.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client (Service A)      AuthZ Server             Service B (Target)
  |                          |                          |
  |  [Holds subject_token    |                          |
  |   from original grant]   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---Token Exchange-------->|                          |
  |  (grant_type=            |                          |
  |   token-exchange,        |                          |
  |   subject_token,         |                          |
  |   subject_token_type,    |                          |
  |   [actor_token],         |                          |
  |   [actor_token_type],    |                          |
  |   audience=service-b,    |                          |
  |   scope=read)            |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--New Access Token-------|                          |
  |  (scoped for service-b,  |                          |
  |   may include act claim) |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---API Request w/ New Token-------------------->|
  |<--Protected Resource-----------------------------|
